blurb:
Mandy was living outside at an apartment complex, doing the hard thing, raising kittens on her own. Her babies have all been adopted. Now it's her turn.

She's about a year and a half old, part Maine Coon, and the kind of cat that flips onto her back and lets you rub her belly without hesitation. Very gentle, very trusting for a cat who started life on the streets. She just wants somewhere warm and permanent to land.

One thing worth knowing: Mandy is FIV positive. She likely picked it up during mating from a bite wound. FIV is only transmitted between cats through a deep bite, not through shared bowls, shared litter boxes, or general contact. Plenty of households have FIV positive and FIV negative cats living together without issue. Her foster does. It's manageable, and it doesn't change who she is — a sweet, belly-rub-loving cat who has already proven she can handle a lot.

Mandy is with Patty's Kitties, a small Atlanta-based rescue. Reach out by phone or email to learn more or set up a time to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Stray-mom-who-raised-kittens-alone arc is emotionally complete and immediately compelling — her babies got homes, now readers can give her one
• FIV section is handled with rare clarity and calm, turning a perceived barrier into a trust-building education moment for the newsletter
• Young, affectionate, belly-rub-tolerant cat with a resilience story is a strong general-audience adoption candidate

blurb:
Sebastian is the one with the head tilt and the energy. He loves attention, plays hard, and will occasionally punctuate his enthusiasm with a little nibble or swat — not mean, just excited. He's the kind of cat who makes you laugh. His brother Ben is the opposite: quieter, takes a bit longer to warm up, but once he does, he's genuinely sweet and enjoys gentle attention. Two brothers, two completely different vibes, and they've been side by side since they came in from a county shelter together.

Bonded pairs can feel like a commitment, but these two actually make the case for themselves. Ben steadies things out when Sebastian gets wound up. Sebastian keeps things from getting too quiet. They've already figured each other out, which means they're not going to spend their first weeks in your home adjusting — they're going to hit the ground running.

Sebastian and Ben need to be adopted together, so you'll want room for two. If you've got it, this is a pretty solid deal.

Good Mews is a no-kill, cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. You'll need to submit an inquiry and visit in person to meet the brothers. Call or email to get started.
scoring_notes:
• The yin-and-yang brother dynamic is an easy, relatable hook that makes the bonded-pair requirement feel like a feature rather than a barrier
• County shelter origin gives a mild rescue narrative, though the blurb lacks a standout individual hardship story to maximize emotional impact
• Bonded-pair status limits the adopter pool and likely extends their stay, adding quiet urgency even without explicit time-in-shelter detail
